and Data Structures

Dr. Desmond Tsoi
Department of Computer Science & Engineering

Hong Kong SAR, China

slides.title: What are References?
slides.raw_text:
What are References?
A reference is an alternative / another name (alias) for a variable or
an object
Reference variables are usually used in parameter passing to functions
Rm 3553, desmond@ust.hk COMP2012 (Fall 2022) 2 / 17

slides.title: Declaration of Reference Variables
Important Points about Reference
slides.raw_text:
Important Points about Reference
A reference MUST always bound to a variable / an object (similar to
constant pointer)
It must therefore be initialized when it is created
int j = 1;
int& r1 = j; // ok
int& r2; // error!
A reference cannot bound to another object once it initially was
initialized to. But what does the following mean?
int j = 10;
int& r = j;
int k = 50;
r = k; // what does this mean?
Assignment from a variable, say k, to a reference variable, say r, means r
get assigned with the v

slides.title: The Different Uses of Operator &
Questions
Example
Call by Reference
Call by Reference
slides.raw_text:
Call by Reference
Variable i is a local variable in
the function f. Its type is “int
reference” and it is created
when f is called
In the call f(j), i is created
similarly to the construction:
int& i = j;
So within the function f, i will
be an alias of the variable j,
and i cannot be binded to
another variable
But every time the function is
called, a new variable i is
created and it can be a reference
to a different variable / object
Rm 3553, desmond@ust.hk COMP2012 (Fall 2022) 9 / 17

slides.title: Why Call by Reference?
slides.raw_text:
Why Call by Reference?
There are two reasons:
1. The function caller wants the
function to be able to change
the value of passed arguments
2. For efficiency:
▶ If you pass a function
argument by value, the
function gets a local copy of
the argument
▶ For large objects, copying is
expensive; on the other hand,
passing an object by reference
does not require copying, only
a memory address (since
reference is similar to pointer)
Rm 3553, desmond@ust.hk COMP2012 (Fall 2022) 10 / 17

slides.title: Example
const: References as Function Arguments
const: References as Function Arguments (Cont’d)
Pointer vs. Reference
slides.raw_text:
Pointer vs. Reference
A reference can be thought of as a special kind of pointer, but there are 3
big differences to remember!
A pointer can point to nothing (nullptr), but a reference is always
bound to a variable / object
▶ No need to check nullptr when using reference ;)
A pointer can point to different variables / objects at different times
(through assignments). A reference is always bound to the same
variable / object.
▶ Assignments to a reference do NOT change the variable / object it
ref

Further Reading
Read Chapter 6 & 8 of ”C++ How to Program” or Chapter 3 of
”C++ Primer” textbook
Rm 3553, desmond@ust.hk COMP2012 (Fall 2022) 16 / 17

topic_segments.transcript_text:
Pointer vs. Reference
A reference can be thought of as a special kind of pointer, but there are 3
big differences to remember!
A pointer can point to nothing (nullptr), but a reference is always
bound to a variable / object
▶ No need to check nullptr when using reference ;)
A pointer can point to different variables / objects at different times
(through assignments). A reference is always bound to the same
variable / object.
▶ Assignments to a reference do NOT change the variable / object it
refers to but only the value of the referenced object
The name of a pointer refers to the pointer variable. The * or − >
(details of operator − > will be covered later) operators have to be
used to access the object. The name of a reference always refers to
the same object. There are no special operators. ;)
Rm 3553, desmond@ust.hk COMP2012 (Fall 2022) 14 / 17

reel_scripts.hook: Did you know variables are just memory addresses? Let's decode references!
reel_scripts.narration_text: References in OOP are like aliases for memory addresses. When you pass a variable to a function, you're actually passing its memory location. This lets functions modify original data. Slide 1 shows our review topic—references are crucial for efficient data handling. Think of them as 'pointers' in C++ but safer. Mastering this helps you avoid copy-paste errors in your code!

visual_directions.description: Zoom in on slide 1's title 'COMP2012 Object-Oriented Programming' and professor's name
Text: 'References = Memory Addresses' with animated arrow pointing to slide's 'Review: References' section
Animated diagram: Variable 'x' pointing to memory box with 'value 10', then arrow to function parameter 'x' modifying the same box

reel_scripts.key_takeaway: References allow functions to modify original data by passing memory addresses
reel_scripts.quiz_question: What is a reference in OOP?
quiz_choices: A) A copy of the variable's value
B) An alias for the variable's memory address
C) A type of loop structure

reel_scripts.hook: Ever wondered how variables share values without copying? Let’s decode REFERENCES!
reel_scripts.narration_text: References are like nicknames for variables or objects. They let you access the same data without making copies. Think of them as aliases—like calling your friend 'Buddy' instead of their real name. In coding, references are super useful for passing data to functions. They save memory and make your code faster. So next time you see a reference, remember: it's not a new variable—it's a shortcut to the original!

visual_directions.description: Zoom in on Slide 2, highlighting the text: 'A reference is an alternative / another name (alias) for a variable or object'
Show two variables (e.g., 'x' and 'y') pointing to the same object, then split them to show 'y' is a reference to 'x'
Display code snippet: 'void func(int& ref) { ref = 10; }' with animated arrows showing reference passing

reel_scripts.key_takeaway: References are aliases that let variables share data without copying.
reel_scripts.quiz_question: What is a reference in programming?

reel_scripts.hook: Did you know references in C++ act like aliases for variables? Let's break it down!
reel_scripts.narration_text: References let you create another name for a variable. The syntax is <type>& <name> = <variable>;. For example, int j = 1; then int& r = j; makes r a nickname for j. When you change r, j changes too! It's like a magical mirror. Assigning r = 2 makes j = 2. Even if you later set j = 10, r still reflects that change. No pointers needed—just simple syntax!

visual_directions.description: Slide 3: Highlight the syntax <type>& <variable name> = <variable>;
Show int j = 1; then int& r = j; with 'j' and 'r' linked by a dotted line
Display code: r = 2; → j = 2; then j = 10; → r = 10;

reel_scripts.key_takeaway: References create aliases for variables, so changes to the reference affect the original value.
reel_scripts.quiz_question: What happens when you set r = 2 after int& r = j;?
quiz_choices: C) Both r and j change

reel_scripts.hook: Did you know references in C++ can't be uninitialized? Let's break it down!
reel_scripts.narration_text: References must always bind to a variable like a constant pointer. You can't create a reference without initializing it—like trying to point a compass without a target! If you assign a new value to a reference, it updates the original variable. For example, if r = k, j gets changed, not r itself. References are fixed once initialized—no switching targets!

visual_directions.description: Show Slide 4 with code: int j=1; int& r1=j; // ok
Animate int j=10; int& r=j; r=50; showing j changes to 50
Display code: int& r2; // error! with red highlighting

reel_scripts.key_takeaway: References must bind to a variable at creation and update the original value when assigned.
reel_scripts.quiz_question: What happens when you do r = k with a reference r initialized to j?
quiz_choices: A) r now points to k
B) j's value changes to k's value
C) Both r and k are reset
reel_scripts.suggested_caption: References in C++ are fixed once initialized! 💡 #CPlusPlus #ProgrammingTips #CodeLikePro

reel_scripts.hook: Did you know the & operator has TWO secrets? Let me explain!
reel_scripts.narration_text: The & operator in C++ has two big roles! First, it declares a reference variable like int& i = j;. Second, it gets the address of a variable like int* p = &j;. Confusing? Let me break it down with code examples!

visual_directions.description: Slide 5: Highlight code examples for reference and address-of operator
Split screen: Left side shows reference declaration, right side shows address-of operator with memory addresses

reel_scripts.key_takeaway: The & operator has two roles: reference declaration and address-of operator.
reel_scripts.quiz_question: What does & do in int& i = j;?

reel_scripts.hook: Why is this code wrong? Let me explain!
reel_scripts.narration_text: This code is wrong because you can't assign a reference to an address. References must point to variables, not memory addresses. But the correct example shows a reference to a pointer. It's like a 'pointer to a pointer' — a reference that points to another pointer. Get it? Let me break it down.

visual_directions.description: Zoom in on Slide 6 showing the incorrect code: 'int& i = &j;'
Highlight the correct code: 'int*& ref = p;' with animation showing pointer layers
Display 'Reference = Alias, Pointer = Address' with fading text

reel_scripts.key_takeaway: References are aliases, pointers track addresses — mixing them creates errors.
reel_scripts.quiz_question: Which code correctly creates a reference to a pointer?
reel_scripts.suggested_caption: Why is this C++ code wrong? 🧠 #CPlusPlus #ProgrammingTips #CodeDebugging

reel_scripts.hook: Did you know changing a pointer can magically update a reference? Let’s break this code!
reel_scripts.narration_text: This C++ example shows a pointer and a reference to that pointer. When we change the pointer to point to a new variable, the reference magically updates too! Let’s see how the code works step by step.

visual_directions.description: Show Slide 7 with the code snippet, zoom in on the line 'int*& ref = pi;' to highlight the reference declaration
Animate the code execution: show variable j=1, pi pointing to j, then pi changing to point to k=2. Highlight how ref always tracks pi
Display output side-by-side: 'j=1, *pi=1, *ref=1' then 'j=1, *pi=2, *ref=2'

reel_scripts.key_takeaway: References to pointers act as aliases - changes to the pointer automatically update the reference.
reel_scripts.quiz_question: What happens when we change the pointer in this example?
quiz_choices: A) The reference remains pointing to the original variable
B) The reference magically updates to the new pointer
D) Both the pointer and reference become null

reel_scripts.hook: Did you know changing a variable in a function can alter the original? Let's break down call by reference!
reel_scripts.narration_text: Call by reference lets functions modify original variables. When you pass a variable as a reference, changes inside the function affect the original. Look at this code: a function increments a reference parameter, then both the return value and the original variable show 8. That's because the function worked directly with the memory address of 'j'!

visual_directions.description: Show Slide 8: Highlight the line 'int f(int& i)' to emphasize the reference parameter
Animate the code execution: show 'j = 7' turning into 'j = 8' after function call
Display output: '8' and '8' side by side with arrows pointing to the original variable

reel_scripts.key_takeaway: Call by reference modifies the original variable by working with its memory address
reel_scripts.quiz_question: What happens when you pass a variable by reference in a function?
quiz_choices: A) The original variable remains unchanged
B) The original variable is modified
C) The function creates a copy of the variable
reel_scripts.suggested_caption: 💡 Call by reference = original variable gets modified! #C++ #Programming #CompSci #CodeTips

reel_scripts.hook: Did you know passing by reference makes variables act like twins?!
reel_scripts.narration_text: When you pass a variable by reference, it's like creating an alias! The function gets a direct link to the original variable. So changes inside the function? They affect the real variable. But here's the twist: every function call creates a new reference, not a new variable. Think of it like a nickname that changes each time you call the function. No binding to other variables, just direct access!

visual_directions.description: Show Slide 9: Highlight the 'int& i = j;' line and the alias concept diagram
Animate variable j and i as twin icons with 'Alias' label connecting them
Display code snippet: 'int& i = j;' with 'Reference' highlighted in red

reel_scripts.key_takeaway: Call by reference creates direct links to original variables, not copies
reel_scripts.quiz_question: What can't be done with a reference variable i in a function?
reel_scripts.suggested_caption: 💡 Call by reference = direct variable linking! #Cplusplus #ProgrammingTips #DevLife

reel_scripts.hook: Did you know passing variables can actually change the original? Let's see why call by reference is a game-changer!
reel_scripts.narration_text: Call by reference lets functions modify original data AND saves memory! First, it lets the function change the value of passed arguments. Second, for efficiency: passing large objects by value copies them entirely, which is slow. But by reference, we just pass the memory address—no copying needed! This is huge for performance.

visual_directions.description: Show Slide 10 with the two bullet points: 'The function caller wants the' and 'For efficiency:'
Animate a 'copying vs. referencing' diagram: show a large object being copied (with a 'cloning' animation) vs. a pointer arrow to the same memory address
Text overlay: 'Pass by value = full copy | Pass by reference = memory address only'

reel_scripts.key_takeaway: Call by reference lets functions modify original data and saves memory by avoiding copies.
reel_scripts.quiz_question: What are the two main reasons for using call by reference?
quiz_choices: A) Modifying original data and efficiency
